articles.title: Modern Times: The Biography of a Hungarian-Jewish Family Stephen Pogany
Who's To Blame: Collective Guilt on Trial , Coline Covington
Autograph albums as Holocaust objects: hapticity, futurity, trauma
articles.abstract: Abstract Within the growing scholarship on creative production inside sites of persecution during the Holocaust, researchers have studied autograph albums to a much lesser extent than diaries and other writing. This article presents three autograph albums that Jewish girls and young women carried with them on their journeys through ghettos and camps, and in hiding. The prewar autograph album genre required owners to circulate these small books to friends and acquaintances to solicit whimsical verse, well wishes for the future, or slogans for moral improvement. This article foregrounds the importance of inscribers physically handling the albums, as well as the role of genre, to argue that autograph albums are best considered objects rather than texts. Scholars have much to gain by considering the materiality of these sources to understand the experiences of girls and women in the Holocaust.

articles.title: AMERICAN REFORM TRANSLATIONS OF THE PASSOVER HAGGADAH: SHIFTING APPROACHES TO THE SH’FOKH ḤAMATKHA SECTION, 1892–2021
articles.abstract: Abstract The Sh’fokh Hamatkha (“Pour out Thy Wrath”) section in the Passover Haggadah, invoking Elijah as a personal Messiah and juxtaposing vengeance on non-Jewish nations with redemptive hope, poses particular ideological challenges to Reform Judaism’s rational and universalistic values. The present article traces how American Reform Haggadot from 1892 to 2021 have translated, omitted, or reframed the Sh’fokh Hamatkha section, historically highlighting these strategies’ underlying theological and educational overtones. Two main approaches are outlined and discussed: the early Reform Haggadot’s elimination or paratextual displacement of the Sh’fokh Hamatkha section; and the more recent tendency to restore both the section’s ritualistic features and its vengeful verses—reinterpreted, annotated, and often paired with alternative readings promoting universal peace and justice. This article argues that the shifting approaches of the editors of American Reform Haggadot evolved from avoidance to engagement, demonstrating an increasing willingness to address the section’s theological challenges in a more direct manner, and to struggle educationally with its implications.

articles.title: POLAND: THE MOST UNPLEASANT TRUTH IS A SAFER COMPANION THAN A PLEASANT FALSEHOOD
articles.abstract: Abstract The authors of this text, two Israeli historians, probe the background to the keynote speech delivered by Thomas Rose, the US ambassador to Poland, at the conference of the International Association of Jewish Lawyers and Judges in Warsaw in November 2025. The newly appointed envoy—a Republican political appointee and former publisher of The Jerusalem Post—insisted that claims of any Polish complicity in the German annihilation of Polish Jewry amount to a “blood libel.” In Contrast, Dreifuss and Weinbaum contend that these remarks serve to reinforce ongoing efforts by individuals and Polish officialdom to delegitimize the rigorous scholarship of a determined band of Polish researchers who have been studying wartime Polish–Jewish relations for nearly three decades. While condemning the occasional conflation of Poles with the actual masterminds and prime perpetrators of the Final Solution, the authors emphasize that local participation in the degradation, dispossession, and destruction of Polish Jews was far more extensive than had been previously recognized. They further argue that both Communist and post-Communist governments, as well as Polish individuals and institutions at home and in exile, have resisted confronting these findings and that scholars and others who challenge more reassuring narratives have faced sustained and often vitriolic attacks. The authors conclude that this excruciating chapter of history must be confronted dispassionately—on the basis of meticulous scholarly inquiry—and should not be subordinated to diplomatic or political agendas.

articles.title: SENATOR FULBRIGHT’S 1963 AIPAC HEARING IN THE SHADOW OF THE JFK ASSASSINATION CONSPIRACY
articles.abstract: Abstract This article examines the 1963 Senate Foreign Relations Committee hearing led by Senator J. William Fulbright, which scrutinized whether the American–Israel Public Affairs Committee's (AIPAC) predecessor should have registered under the Foreign Agents Registration Act, and its implications for U.S. political lobbying and foreign influence regulation. In light of the recent public release of the JFK assassination files by President Donald Trump, which have been exploited by antisemitic conspiracy theorists to falsely claim that AIPAC orchestrated President Kennedy’s assassination to avoid losing its legal status, this article situates the 1963 hearing within a broader historical debate over AIPAC’s identity as a domestic American organization versus a foreign agent. The study engages with archival records, contemporary media coverage, and congressional transcripts to provide a detailed, evidence-based analysis of the political, legal, and diplomatic dynamics surrounding the Fulbright hearing and its lasting significance for U.S.–Israel relations.
